text:
This study investigated the reactive partitioning of cis- and trans-β-IEPOX onto hydrated inorganic seed particles in the absence of any added acids. No organic aerosol (OA) was formed on dry ammonium sulfate (AS). However, both isomers reacted to form OA very quickly on wet AS seeds across liquid water mass fractions ranging from 40% to 75%. The formation of the observed OA was determined to be a kinetically controlled process with the growth continuing as long as the gas-phase IEPOX was available. There were no differences in the growth rates or the composition of the products between the cis and trans isomers.

High OA loadings were also obtained on hydrated AS seeds that were treated with base (NaOH). The pH dependence of the IEPOX reactive uptake appears relatively weak for ammonium containing particles. The OA was however completely volatilized upon drying the particle when Na+ is used instead of NH4+. The Henry's Law constant of IEPOX with NaCl particles, at an ionic strength of about 9 M, is 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹ (−50 / +100%). A much weaker nucleophilicity of the sulfur atom in sulfate as compared to the chloride one in chloride replacing sulfate experiments, led to very low (less than 5%) OA formation.

Composition analysis from online aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS) and offline filter extracts show high concentrations of oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates and amines. These results match expectations from field measurements of poor correlation between IEPOX-derived OA and acidity or liquid water in SOA, suggesting that many conditions limit the catalytic process to nucleophile availability rather than water or activity.
explanation: The passage is highly domain-specific with coherent experimental details and numbers, which could come from a human author; however, some unusual phrasing (e.g., the parenthetical '(-50 / +100%)') introduces ambiguity.
